text:
I feel that the LEGO analogy has maybe muddied the water more than clarified it? I don't find it a useful way to think about software: <https://crabmusket.net/lego-blocks-are-never-a-good-analogy/|https://crabmusket.net/lego-blocks-are-never-a-good-analogy/> (Basically, LEGO bricks have a simple interface, and uniform purpose. Software rarely has a simple interface, and components of a system do not have uniform purpose.)

I am interested in what software might look like if built around the premises you describe- how similar is it to the actor model? Lots of elements of what you're talking about seem similar to that.

I've long since left the serious FP world behind. But, you can wrest my TypeScript flow-based structural typing from my unwilling hands. To me, the benefits of learning Haskell were to think seriously about purity, and declarative syntax.

I've long since left the serious FP world behind. But, you can wrest my TypeScript flow-based structural typing from my unwilling hands. To me, the benefits of learning Haskell were to think seriously about purity, and declarative syntax.

text:
A bit harsh for functional programming (as already stated by others), but it summarizes nicely my own feelings toward the current crop of (mainstream) programming language research. We shouldn't dismiss type theory altogether, but should definitely stop with the oversophisticated type systems, and should rethink over the computational substrate/notation they are founded upon.

I think there is a lot of interesting work in this space with string diagrams, if one still wants the mathematical beauty and rigor. But we lack a standard way to represent blackbox abstraction, which is really the purpose that we force upon and expect from functions. We also need more « science popularization » to get this out of the hands of mathematicians who focus too much on (denotational) semantics, and into the hands of engineers designing languages for real-world computations. Might take years or decades though, notably requiring some huge innovations in visual programming environments (text-only is too limited IMO).

text:
In <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oKg1hTOQXoY&t=2121s|The Computer Revolution Hasn't Happened Yet> Alan Kay says "...problem finding is more important than problem solving...". A problem, as I see it, is "software doesn't just snap together like LEGO blocks". Carefully fitting gears together isn't the same as snapping parts together, IMO. If that's the problem, then one needs only to make a list of the reasons and to systematically solve each of them. I think some of these are:
• synchronous behaviour (this is *the* definition of the synchronous/sequential programming paradigm, inspired by sequencer ICs (aka "CPUs"), not ideas like the internet or robotics or IoT or etc), 
• routing (FP dictates one and only one routing policy - sequential, blocking), 
• fan-out (FP forbids it, UNIX doesn't support it (you have to explicitly add "tee"), FBP forbids it), 
• lack of recursive definition of software units for simplification by abstraction, over-complicated types for input and output signatures, 
• lack of layering of types (I can't think of what a good phrase/word for this is, something akin to the OSI-7 layer model for networking and wrapping data in a Russian doll fashion (simple transport layer on the outside, more complication as successive layers are peeled off))
Many of these problems have been attacked by force-fitting baubles into the functional paradigm. Now that it has become easy to build new syntaxes (PEG, OhmJS, drawio) I favour using a hybrid of languages instead of stretching the functional paradigm even further out of shape. I have lots of working examples of software built this way, but due to my interest in language design, many of the examples are kinda heavy. Maybe the simplest example is a 3-part <https://programmingsimplicity.substack.com/p/pbp-llm-automated-problem-discovery?r=1egdky|LLM thingie> I threw together on a whim (that feedback loop is not "recursion"). Every implementation of Actors that I've seen has been polluted by the functional mindset. I find the PBP manifestation to be friendlier.

text:
This has been a long time in the making - but my book *Cultures of Programming* is out (as open-access): <https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/cultures-of-programming/075A2D1DE611EE47807A683147B21691>

It looks at the history of programming as interaction between five different cultures (hacker, engineering, mathematical, managerial, humanistic) that look at programming in different ways and have different basic ideas about what programs are and how to best construct them.

One interesting observation from the book is that the humanistic culture (which includes Engelbart, Kay, Papert and many other favourite references here :slightly_smiling_face: ) seems to historically be the one that comes up with interesting ideas, but those are later adopted by more pragmatic engineering/managerial cultures and transformed in a way that removes what makes them unique (OO turns from medium for thinking into a controlled engineering tool). Alas, I do not have any historical lessons suggesting how to go against this trend!

text: Maybe what's missing today is institutions that do research on PLs, and more generally software infrastructure, from an applications perspective. Something like "ink & switch but for infrastructure". Today's academic research is not set up for this. It focuses on exploring individual aspects in idealized settings. Which is useful: as <@U096YUBC3KP> said, _learning_ Haskell is valuable even if it's not a great choice for real-life applications. But it's not enough.
